narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application like they're piecing together the story of your journey to the bar exam, drawing from patterns they've seen in thousands of students just like you. Your Year 3 decile of 2 stood out as the biggest chapter pulling the narrative toward struggle—it was the strongest signal that passing the bar might be an uphill battle, even if you'd climbed a bit from your Year 1 decile of 1. Layered on top, your LSAT score of 35 whispered doubts about the raw analytical firepower needed to master those intense exams. Your upper-middle family income, while solid, didn't quite counter the trend in a way that reassured them, nor did your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, which added to the picture of consistent hurdles. As a white male going part-time, these details all nudged the story further toward the risk of failure, painting a composite where the odds tipped to about a 45% chance of not making it across the finish line. It's not that any one piece defined you—your passion for law shines through in targeting this path—but together, they formed a cautionary tale the model couldn't ignore. This isn't a judgment on your potential; it's just the honest lens they used to protect their program's success rate. You've got time to rewrite this story—maybe by boosting those deciles or rethinking part-time demands. We're rooting for you to prove them wrong and pass that bar with flying colors. What small step can you take today toward a stronger next chapter?
